problems.title: Shared Hobby Finder
problems.description:
Two friends are comparing their hobbies to see what they have in common and what makes each of them unique. Write a program that takes both of their hobby lists and figures out:
1. The **full collection** of hobbies between both friends — every hobby at least one of them has.
2. The hobbies they **both enjoy** — things they can do together.
3. Hobbies that only **Friend A** has — things Friend A does alone.
4. Hobbies that only **Friend B** has — things Friend B does alone.

All results must be printed in **alphabetical order**.
constraints: Each friend has between 1 and 10 hobbies.
Hobby names are single words, all lowercase.
Each friend's list has no duplicates.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: Hobbies of **Friend A**, separated by spaces.
Line 2: Hobbies of **Friend B**, separated by spaces.
problems.outputFormat:
Line 1: `All hobbies:` followed by every hobby sorted, separated by commas and a space.
Line 2: `Both enjoy:` followed by shared hobbies sorted. If none, print `Both enjoy: none`.
Line 3: `Only A:` followed by Friend A's unique hobbies sorted. If none, print `Only A: none`.
Line 4: `Only B:` followed by Friend B's unique hobbies sorted. If none, print `Only B: none`.
problems.hint:
a = set(input().split())
b = set(input().split())
print('All hobbies:', ', '.join(sorted(a | b)))
both = sorted(a & b)
print('Both enjoy:', ', '.join(both) if both else 'none')
only_a = sorted(a - b)
print('Only A:', ', '.join(only_a) if only_a else 'none')
only_b = sorted(b - a)
print('Only B:', ', '.join(only_b) if only_b else 'none')

testCases.label: Example 1
expected: All hobbies: cooking, gaming, hiking, reading

testCases.label: Example 2 — no shared hobbies
expected: All hobbies: chess, cooking, dancing, painting, tennis, yoga

testCases.label: Hidden: A's hobbies all inside B
expected: All hobbies: cooking, drawing, gaming, reading, travel

testCases.label: Hidden: One shared, many unique
expected: All hobbies: archery, bowling, fishing, skating, surfing

testCases.label: Hidden: Larger lists, partial overlap
inputs: running cycling swimming yoga chess painting
expected: All hobbies: boxing, chess, cycling, diving, painting, rowing, running, swimming, yoga
Only A: cycling, painting, running, swimming

problems.title: Event Guest Tracker
problems.description:
You are organizing a school event and keeping track of attendees. The sign-in sheet may have some names written more than once by mistake. Write a program that:
1. Reads the sign-in sheet (which may have duplicate names).
2. Converts it to a **set** to count only unique attendees.
3. Prints how many **unique guests** showed up.
4. Reads a **late arrival** name to add to the list.
5. Reads a name to **remove** from the list (the person left early — don't crash if the name isn't there).
6. Prints the **final sorted guest list**.
constraints: The sign-in sheet has between 1 and 20 names.
Names are single words starting with an uppercase letter.
The late arrival name may already be on the list.
The name to remove may or may not be on the list.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: Names from the sign-in sheet, separated by spaces (may have duplicates).
Line 2: A name to **add** (late arrival).
Line 3: A name to **remove** (left early).
problems.outputFormat:
Line 1: `Unique guests:` followed by the count of unique attendees (before adding/removing).
Line 2: `Final list:` followed by the sorted final guest names, separated by commas and a space.
problems.hint:
sheet = input().split()
late   = input().strip()
left   = input().strip()
guests = set(sheet)
print('Unique guests:', len(guests))
guests.add(late)
guests.discard(left)
print('Final list:', ', '.join(sorted(guests)))

testCases.label: Example 1
inputs: Marco Lea Marco Toni Lea Lea

testCases.label: Hidden: No duplicates on sheet
expected: Final list: Dan, Eve, Grace, Hana

testCases.label: Hidden: Remove name not on list
expected: Final list: Alice, Ben, Carl, Diana

testCases.label: Hidden: Many duplicates, small unique set
inputs: Rex Rex Mia Mia Lena Lena

testCases.label: Hidden: Add and remove same name

problems.title: Club Membership Checker
problems.description:
A student club has a list of requirements that members must complete before joining an advanced committee. Write a program that checks a student's completed activities against the requirements:
1. Read the **required activities** to qualify.
2. Read the activities the **student has completed**.
3. Check if the student has completed **all requirements** (i.e., requirements are a subset of completed).
4. If qualified, print a success message.
5. If not, print which **requirements are still missing** (sorted).
6. Finally, print the **non-overlapping activities** — things in one list but not the other — sorted. If none, print `none`.
constraints: Each list has between 1 and 10 activity names.
Activity names are single words, all lowercase.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: Required activities separated by spaces.
Line 2: Student's completed activities separated by spaces.
problems.outputFormat:
Line 1: `Eligible: yes` if all requirements are met, otherwise `Eligible: no`.
Line 2 (only if not eligible): `Missing:` followed by the sorted missing activities, separated by commas and a space.
Last line: `Non-overlapping:` followed by sorted activities in one list but not the other. If none, print `Non-overlapping: none`.
problems.hint:
required  = set(input().split())
done      = set(input().split())
if required.issubset(done):
    print('Eligible: yes')
else:
    print('Eligible: no')
    missing = sorted(required - done)
    print('Missing:', ', '.join(missing))
diff = sorted(required.symmetric_difference(done))
print('Non-overlapping:', ', '.join(diff) if diff else 'none')

testCases.label: Hidden: Completely different lists
expected: Non-overlapping: camping, firstaid, fundraising, leadership, sportsday, teamwork

problems.title: [new] Duplicate Remover & Score Filter
problems.description:
You are processing a raw list of student scores that may contain duplicates. Write a program that:
1. Read a space-separated list of integers (scores, may have duplicates).
2. Convert to a set to get **unique scores**.
3. Use `map()` to create a new set with each unique score **multiplied by 2** (doubled).
4. Use `filter()` to keep only scores from the **original unique set** that are **75 or above** (passing).
5. Use `filter()` to keep only scores from the **original unique set** that are **below 75** (failing).
6. Check if a queried score (read from input) is in the **original unique set** using `in`.
7. Print all results.
constraints: Input scores are integers between 0 and 100.
There are between 3 and 15 scores on the input line.
The query score is a single integer.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: Space-separated integers (raw scores with possible duplicates).
Line 2: A single integer to check membership.
problems.outputFormat:
Line 1: `Unique: <n>` — count of unique scores.
Line 2: `Doubled: <sorted list>` — sorted list of doubled unique scores.
Line 3: `Passing: <sorted list>` — sorted list of unique scores >= 75.
Line 4: `Failing: <sorted list>` — sorted list of unique scores < 75.
Line 5: `Found` if the query score is in unique scores, otherwise `Not found`.
problems.hint:
raw = list(map(int, input().split()))
query = int(input())
unique = set(raw)
doubled = sorted(set(map(lambda x: x * 2, unique)))
passing = sorted(filter(lambda x: x >= 75, unique))
failing = sorted(filter(lambda x: x < 75, unique))
print('Unique:', len(unique))
print('Doubled:', doubled)
print('Passing:', passing)
print('Failing:', failing)
print('Found' if query in unique else 'Not found')

testCases.label: Example 2 — mixed passing/failing, query not found

problems.title: [new] Permission Role Mapper
problems.description:
You are building an access control system using **frozensets as dictionary keys**. The system maps sets of permissions to role names.

Your program should:
1. The system has three fixed roles stored as a dictionary with **frozenset keys**:
   - `frozenset({'read'})` → `'Viewer'`
   - `frozenset({'read', 'write'})` → `'Editor'`
   - `frozenset({'read', 'write', 'delete'})` → `'Admin'`
2. Read a comma-separated list of permissions for **User A**.
3. Read a comma-separated list of permissions for **User B**.
4. For each user: look up their role. If no match, print `'Unknown'`.
5. Use `update()` to give User A all of User B's permissions, then look up the new role.
6. Find the **intersection** of User A's original and User B's permissions (shared permissions), print sorted.
7. Find permissions **only User B has** (difference B - A original), print sorted.
8. Check using `isdisjoint()` whether the two original permission sets share nothing.
constraints: Permission names are lowercase single words.
Each user has between 1 and 3 permissions, no duplicates per user.
Inputs are comma-separated with no extra spaces.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: Comma-separated permissions for User A.
Line 2: Comma-separated permissions for User B.
problems.outputFormat:
Line 1: `User A role: <role>`
Line 2: `User B role: <role>`
Line 3: `Combined role: <role>` — role after A gains B's permissions
Line 4: `Shared: <sorted list>` — permissions in both
Line 5: `Only B: <sorted list>` — permissions only B has (empty list if none)
Line 6: `Disjoint: <True/False>`
problems.hint:
roles = {
    frozenset({'read'}): 'Viewer',
    frozenset({'read', 'write'}): 'Editor',
    frozenset({'read', 'write', 'delete'}): 'Admin'
}
perms_a = set(input().split(','))
perms_b = set(input().split(','))
print('User A role:', roles.get(frozenset(perms_a), 'Unknown'))
print('User B role:', roles.get(frozenset(perms_b), 'Unknown'))
combined = perms_a | perms_b
print('Combined role:', roles.get(frozenset(combined), 'Unknown'))
print('Shared:', sorted(perms_a & perms_b))
print('Only B:', sorted(perms_b - perms_a))
print('Disjoint:', perms_a.isdisjoint(perms_b))

testCases.label: Hidden: Unknown role A, known role B
